questions.question: What is the primary visual metaphor used by board view databases in Notion to represent individual items?

options.text: Rows
options.explanation: Rows are typical of table views, not board views.

options.text: Cards
options.explanation: Each entry appears as a card, which can be moved between columns.

options.text: Lists
options.explanation: Lists are a different view type; board view uses cards.

options.text: Tiles
options.explanation: Tiles are not the terminology used for board view items.

questions.question: Which of the following actions can you perform directly on a board view card?

options.text: Change the page's URL
options.explanation: URL changes are not a card‑level action.

options.text: Show a preview of the entry’s content
options.explanation: Boards allow a preview of a card’s content to be displayed.

options.text: Convert the board into a calendar automatically
options.explanation: Conversion requires a separate view change, not a card action.

options.text: Assign a password to the card
options.explanation: Passwords are not a feature of board view cards.

questions.question: How can you add a new board view to an existing Notion page?

options.text: Click the 'New page' button and select 'Board' from the dropdown
options.explanation: Boards can be added via the New page button or by typing '/Board'.

options.text: Drag a file from your desktop onto the page
options.explanation: Dragging a file creates a file block, not a board view.

options.text: Right‑click any text block and choose 'Convert to Board'
options.explanation: Conversion options are limited to certain view types, not directly to board.

options.text: Upload a CSV and select 'Board' as the import format
options.explanation: CSV import creates a table, not a board view.

questions.question: When organizing cards in a board view, which property can be used to automatically group cards into columns?

options.text: Title
options.explanation: The title is displayed on each card but does not define column grouping.

options.text: Select or multi‑select property
options.explanation: Boards can be grouped by any property, commonly a select or multi‑select field.

options.text: Created time
options.explanation: Created time can be displayed but isn’t used for column grouping by default.

options.text: Page icon
options.explanation: Icons are visual only and don’t affect column organization.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using Notion’s template gallery when creating a board view?

options.text: It automatically syncs the board with external project‑management tools
options.explanation: Templates do not provide automatic external syncing.

options.text: It provides pre‑designed board structures that can be customized for your workflow
options.explanation: Templates give you a ready‑made board layout you can adapt.

options.text: It locks the board so no one else can edit it
options.explanation: Templates have no effect on permissions.

options.text: It converts the board into a kanban chart that updates in real time
options.explanation: Boards are already kanban‑style; templates just provide starting content.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about manually rearranging cards in a board view is true?

options.text: Cards can only be moved within the same column
options.explanation: Cards can be dragged across columns as well as within a column.

options.text: Rearranging cards does not affect the underlying database order
options.explanation: Board view ordering is visual; the database order remains unchanged unless you modify a property.

options.text: Moving a card automatically changes its page title
options.explanation: Moving cards changes only their position, not their titles.

options.text: You must hold the Shift key to move a card between columns
options.explanation: No modifier key is required; drag‑and‑drop works directly.

questions.question: A team wants to track the progress of multiple projects, each with several phases, using a board view. They need to see at a glance which phase each project is in, while also being able to filter by project owner. Which configuration best satisfies these requirements?

options.text: Group cards by the 'Phase' select property, place each phase in its own column, and add a filter where 'Owner' equals the current user.
options.explanation: Grouping by Phase creates columns for each stage, and filtering by Owner shows only the relevant projects for each user.

options.text: Create separate boards for each project owner and manually move cards between them as phases change.
options.explanation: Separate boards duplicate effort and lose the unified view of phases.

options.text: Use a single column and sort cards by 'Owner', then manually rename column titles to match phases.
options.explanation: A single column does not provide a visual stage layout; renaming columns manually defeats dynamic grouping.

options.text: Group cards by 'Owner' and add a separate view that sorts by 'Phase' without filtering.
options.explanation: Grouping by Owner shows owners as columns, not phases, which does not meet the requirement to visualize phases.
